titlestmt.titleproper: Guide to the W. S. Lewis Collection of Marie du Deffand
titlestmt.author: by Eileen A. Horansky

controlnote.p: This collection contains 1 published digital object. And that object are comprised of 35 digital files.

descriptivenote.p: Finding aid description and metadata in Archives at Yale are licensed under a CC0 1.0 Universal (CC0 1.0) Public Domain Dedication. This means that anyone may copy, modify, and distribute this descriptive metadata without restriction or permission. The CC0 license is explained in full on the Creative Commons website: CC0 1.0 Universal (CC0 1.0) Public Domain Dedication. More information is available on our Help page.

maintenanceevent.eventdescription: This finding aid was produced using ArchivesSpace on Thursday September 26, 2024 at 01:00

did.unittitle: W. S. Lewis Collection of Marie du Deffand

descriptivenote.p: Chiefly in French with some materials in English.

did.abstract: The W. S. Lewis Collection of Marie du Deffand contains correspondence and other papers created, owned by, or relating to Marie Anne de Vichy Chamrond, marquise du Deffand (1697-1780), a friend and correspondent of the English author Horace Walpole. The collection was compiled by author, editor, and collector Wilmarth S. Lewis (1895-1979). It includes letters exchanged between du Deffand and her wide circle of friends and acquaintances, manuscripts including verses, essays, memoirs, word portraits, and songs by du Deffand or members of her circle, and later materials relating to du Deffand's nineteenth-century editors.

accessrestrict.head: Conditions Governing Access
accessrestrict.p: This collection is open for research.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Organized into three series: I. Correspondence, 1706-1830. II. Personal Papers, 1779-1780. III. Other Papers, 1710-1858.

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: Marie de Vichy Chamrond, marquise du Deffand (1697-1780) is remembered as one of the foremost salonnières of Paris in the mid-eighteenth century. In 1718 at the age of 21, du Deffand was married to her cousin Jean Baptiste de la Lande, marquis du Deffand, but the couple separated within three years. Afterward, du Deffand immersed herself in life at court and carried on several affairs, even briefly becoming the mistress of the Regent Philippe II, duc d'Orléans (1725-1785), before forming what would become a long-term attachment with Charles-Jean-François Hénault (1685-1770), president of the Chambre des Enquêtes. Following her separation, du Deffand was supported by income from a variety of sources, including pensions obtained on her behalf by friends, inheritances from both her grandmother Marie Bouthillier, duchesse de Choiseul (1646-1728), and her aunt Marie Brulart, duchesse de Luynes (1685?-1763), as well as the reversion of her dowry upon the death of her husband in 1750.

Known for her keen intellect and sharp wit, Marie du Deffand entertained a small circle of friends at her winter quarters on the Rue de Beaune and at the court of the duchesse du Maine, before expanding her salon in 1747 to a rented apartment in the Convent of the Filles de St-Joseph. There she attracted political leaders from across Europe, entertaining such luminaries as Voltaire, (1694-1778), Charles de Secondat, baron de Montesquieu (1689-1755), Marguerite-Jeanne, baronne de Staal (1684-1750), Jean Le Rond d'Alembert (1717-1783), and Hénault. In its later years in particular, her salon gained a reputation as a center of wit and gossip.

One of the most consequential figures in du Deffand's life was Julie de Lespinasse (1732-1776), the illegitimate daughter of her brother Gaspard-Nicolas de Vichy, comte de Champrond (1699-1781). When du Deffand began to lose her eyesight in 1752, she invited her niece to Paris to live as her companion. By the time Lespinasse arrived in 1754, du Deffand had become totally blind. After several years the relationship between the two women eventually deteriorated, and in 1764 Lespinasse left to start her own rival salon, taking with her many of her aunt's more literary and intellectual followers. Although several of du Deffand's aristocratic friends remained in her salon, she deeply felt her niece's betrayal.

An important and constant presence in du Deffand's life, especially after the loss of her sight, was Jean-François Wiart, who served as her secretary for nearly thirty years. Another principal relationship was her friendship with the English antiquarian, collector, and author Horace Walpole (1717-1797). Although du Deffand never traveled to see Walpole in England, he visited her regularly in France between 1765 and 1775, and the two are estimated to have exchanged nearly two thousand letters between them. Marie du Deffand spent her final years surrounded by a smaller yet devoted group of friends; she died in Paris in 1780. Such was her regard for Walpole that du Deffand left her collection of papers—as well as her dog Tonton—to him upon her death.

custodhist.head: Custodial History
custodhist.p: Marie du Deffand bequeathed her papers to her friend Horace Walpole, who kept them during his lifetime in a cedar chest at his home, Strawberry Hill, in Twickenham. The papers remained at Strawberry Hill following Walpole's death, until they were sold in 1842 to D. O. Dyce-Sombre as Lot 107 on the sixth day of the great Strawberry Hill sale. In 1920, the son of Dyce-Sombre's nephew W. R. Parker Jervis sold the papers, which were divided into eight individual lots, in a March 10-12 Sotheby's sale. The antiquarian book and manuscripts firm Maggs Bros. purchased three of the lots (390, 392, and 394) and later sold them to Wilmarth S. Lewis. Three lots (388, 391, and 393) initially went to others but were eventually acquired by Lewis. The two remaining lots are now at the Bodleian Library, University of Oxford (387) and the Bibliotheque National (389).

While most of the material in this collection was at one point owned by Horace Walpole, not all of du Deffand's papers made it into Walpole's hands after her death. Madame de Choiseul and Abbé Barthélemy removed their own correspondence from du Deffand's home before her papers passed to Walpole, an action that caused a rift between them and Walpole (see the Yale Edition of Horace Walpole's Correspondence , volume 3). Walpole, in turn, bequeathed his correspondence with du Deffand to Mary Berry, who published a selection in her 1810 edition of du Deffand's correspondence and likely destroyed many of the letters. According to the Yale edition, the marquis de Sainte-Aulaire acquired several of du Deffand's manuscripts, including letters of madame de Choiseul and Abbé Barthélemy, from comte Joseph d'Estourmel, although it is unclear where or how Estourmel acquired these papers. Sainte-Aulaire used these letters for his 1859 edition of du Deffand's correspondence.

Once the du Deffand material arrived at Wilmarth Lewis's library (after 1980, Yale University's Lewis Walpole Library), various call numbers were assigned, including 49 2545 [volumes] I-V, which are listed in Allen T. Hazen's A Catalogue of Horace Walpole's Library , 49 2545 [volume] VI, LWL MSS 11, and LWL MSS Vol. 137. Material under call number 49 2545 VI, a portfolio of loose letters owned by Sainte-Aulaire, had formerly been cataloged as MS. Vol. 138. It was reclassed to 49 2545 VI because evidence, including annotations in Walpole's hand, points to the fact that it belonged to Walpole although it is not included in Hazen's catalogue of Walpole's library.

Whether or not Walpole once owned Sainte-Aulaire's collection of du Deffand's correspondence is unclear. Lewis purchased Sainte-Aulaire's collection from Henry Sorensen in 1950, and correspondence between Lewis and Sorensen indicates that Lewis did believe that the letters had been in Walpole's possession. This conclusion is supported by the presence of a penciled "cross-crosslet," a mark he associated with Walpole, on the two letters from du Deffand to Walpole in this group. This mark is explained in an unsigned letter to the dealer Henry Sorensen in 1971 (likely from Lewis), located in the object file for LWL MSS 11. However, the 1939 Yale Walpole edition identifies this mark as an editorial mark of Mary Berry, who did possess these letters. An undated letter from Warren Hunting Smith also supports the idea that these letters did not actually pass to Walpole and were thus never part of the Strawberry Hill sale or the Parker Jervis sale and did not pass through the hands of Maggs Bros.

A set of typed cards was made to index volumes 49 2545 III-V and LWL MSS 11 by title or correspondent, while cards handwritten by the Yale edition editor Warren Hunting Smith index volume 49 2545 VI by correspondent and selected subjects or contents of each letter, but only offer minimal descriptive information. Additionally, the cards for LWL MSS 11 bear red flags to denote specific Walpole annotations and marks. Volumes 49 2545 I-II and LWL MSS Vol. 137 have title cards but were not further indexed. The catalog cards with their original call numbers have been preserved and may be consulted in the reading room.

A preliminary finding aid covering the material in LWL MSS 11 and volumes 49 2545 I-VI was completed by library staff in 2015, with contents extracted from the library's catalog cards. In summer 2021, all manuscript material once owned by Marie du Deffand was combined under the single call number LWL MSS 11. The reorganization provided the opportunity to arrange and describe all the correspondence and writings by author, and for the staff to research the authors of previously unidentified manuscript fragments of verse and prose. The entire collection was rehoused. Many of the items had been stored in acidic folders bearing manuscript notes in the hand of Wilmarth Lewis's librarian Catherine Jestin (1920-2004), who had stayed on at the library after his death to work for Yale University. In 2021 her notes were photocopied onto archival paper and annotated with the 2015 box and folder numbers; those photocopied notes remain with the relevant items in their new folders.

prefercite.head: Preferred Citation
prefercite.p: W. S. Lewis Collection of Marie du Deffand. The Lewis Walpole Library, Yale University.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The papers in the W. S. Lewis Collection of Marie du Deffand provide a view into the personal life and relationships of a well-connected woman in eighteenth-century France. Although du Deffand had little formal education, her intelligence and wit attracted philosophers, intellectuals, and politicians to her circle, and her papers reflect the breadth and depth of her acquaintance. The collection is particularly rich in correspondence. With over one hundred letters sent between them, her friends Louise Honorine Crozat, duchesse de Choiseul (1734-1801), l'Abbé Barthélemy (1716-1795), and Marguerite-Jeanne, baronne de Staal (1684-1750) are the most well-represented, but also of note is her correspondence with Etienne-François, duc de Choiseul (1719-1785), Jean-Baptiste-Nicolas de Formont (-1758), Jean-Baptiste-Nicholas, chevalier de L'Isle (-1784), Charles de Secondat, baron de Montesquieu, (1689-1755), Carl Fredrik Scheffer (1715-1786), Voltaire (1694-1778), and Horace Walpole (1717-1797). The third-party letters in the collection demonstrate du Deffand's interest in the relationships among other members of her circle, including Jean Le Rond d'Alembert (1717-1783), Stanislas-Jean de Boufflers (1738-1815), Frederick II, King of Prussia, (1712-1786), Charles-Jean-François Hénault (1685-1770), Jean-Jacques Rousseau (1712-1778), and Marie-Anne de La Trémoille, princesse des Ursins (1642-1722).

In addition to the correspondence, du Deffand's papers include her journal and her iconic character word portraits. The word portraits she drew of her friends and acquaintances offer unique insight into some of the prominent figures of the eighteenth century, colored by du Deffand's witty observations. In addition to these documents, the collection also contains a variety of writings from both identified and unidentified authors, including songs, verses, and extracts of works by Voltaire, Jean-Jacques Rousseau, and Stanislas-Jean de Boufflers, presumably made for du Deffand's reference. In total, these papers illustrate the breadth of her intellectual interests and offer insight into du Deffand's personal life as one of the foremost salonnières of Paris in the eighteenth century.

Most of the manuscripts in the collection are in the hand of du Deffand's secretary, Jean-François Wiart, unless otherwise noted. Some hands were identified based on previous research by du Deffand's nineteenth-century editor the marquis de Sainte-Aulaire, and in the twentieth century, Yale University's Warren Hunting Smith and Catherine Jestin. Handwriting that remains unidentified or is surmised is noted as such. Catherine Jestin did much to identify the individuals associated with the papers in the collection, and the organization of this collection is based on her work.

For the most complete description of the Marie du Deffand Collection, see Volume 3 in the Yale Edition of Horace Walpole's Correspondence (New Haven: Yale University Press, 1939), the first of the six volumes in that series devoted to Walpole's relationship with du Deffand, her bequest of her papers to him, and the subsequent history of the material.

userestrict.head: Conditions Governing Use
userestrict.p: The W. S. Lewis Collection of Marie du Deffand is the physical property of the Lewis Walpole Library, Yale University. Literary rights, including copyright, belong to the authors or their legal heirs and assigns. For further information, consult the W. S. Lewis Librarian/Executive Director.

persname.part: Du Deffand, Marie de Vichy Chamrond, marquise, 1697-1780
Choiseul, Louise Honorine Crozat, duchesse de, 1734-1801
Marie Leszczyńska, Queen, consort of Louis XV, King of France, 1703-1768
Montesquieu, Charles de Secondat, baron de, 1689-1755
Sainte-Aulaire, comte de (Louis Clair de Beaupoil), 1778-1854
Champrond, Gaspard-Nicolas de Vichy, comte de (1699-1781)
Vintimille du Luc, Pauline de Mailly-Nesle, marquise de, 1712-1741

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Series I contains the personal correspondence of Marie de Vichy Chamrond, marquise du Deffand, as well as a group of her friends letters she collected or had transcribed. The first subseries, Personal Correspondence, holds letters du Deffand sent and received. The second subseries, Third-party Correspondence, is comprised of letters between other people that she acquired or had copied for her collection.

Once she began to lose her sight in the early 1750s, du Deffand dictated her outgoing letters and other writings to her secretary Jean-François Wiart, although she occasionally called upon her footman Colmant to serve as scribe. After Colmant's death in 1778, du Deffand was assisted by Pétry, a soldier from l'Hôtel des Invalides, and her footmen St.-Jean and Common. This subseries contains both autograph letters by Wiart and copies of others' letters made by him, some by Colmant, and some made by unidentified hands that may belong to these later assistants. Thus, though Series I does contain many original letters written by du Deffand and her correspondents, much of the material here is in the hand of Wiart, unless otherwise noted at the folder level. Wiart also grouped letters into paper wrappers with a note identifying the correspondents.

The series also holds two bound volumes of letters. Box 2 contains a book with the only five known surviving letters written by Horace Walpole to Marie du Deffand in his own hand. The "Recueil de lettres" volume in Box 7 has 414 pages of du Deffand's personal and third-party correspondence as transcribed by Wiart. It is a large quarto album bound in vellum, with numbered pages ruled in red, although there is some inconsistency in numbering; a four-page index appears at the end. The letters were transcribed neither in chronological order nor alphabetically by correspondent. There are pencil annotations throughout: Some letters are marked with a capital "B," possibly placed on them by du Deffand's compiler/editor the marquis de Sainte-Aulaire, to designate that they were published in Mary Berry's edition, and some bear a symbol resembling a capital "H" or a hash mark (its meaning is unknown). Several letters were annotated—sometimes extensively—by Horace Walpole. Others feature the pencil cross-crosslet, also seen on other materials in this collection. This cross-crosslet mark is usually associated with Walpole, but it is also possibly one of Mary Berry's editorial marks. This volume also shows evidence of another kind of editorship in that several leaves have been excised, possibly by the Prince de Beauvau [a comparison with the index shows his letters were the ones removed], leaving only page stubs and mentions in the index as clues to what was removed. The letters in this volume are not listed together as a group in this finding aid, but rather are itemized under the name of each correspondent, with box and volume page numbers noted.

Also in this series is a group of sixty-two letters that were formerly in the possession of the marquis de Sainte-Aulaire; they were acquired by Wilmarth Lewis in 1950 and have been filed under each correspondent's name. They are identified at the item level with an asterisk (*).

Notably absent from this collection are Marie du Deffand's letters to Horace Walpole. These letters were bound in fourteen volumes by Maltby of Oxford for W. R. Parker Jervis and were sold at the Parker Jervis sale in 1920 (lot 387); they are now in the Bodleian Library, University of Oxford. Du Deffand's letters to Walpole have been published in several editions over the centuries, first shortly after Walpole's death by Mary Berry in 1810, later by the marquis de Sainte-Aulaire in 1859 (revised edition, 1866), and notably again by Helen (Mrs. Paget) Toynbee in 1912. The bulk of letters from Walpole to Marie du Deffand were either destroyed by her or returned to him and subsequently destroyed by Mary Berry once she had gleaned pieces of them for her edition of the correspondence. In the mid-twentieth century, Wilmarth S. Lewis undertook a comprehensive publication of the surviving Walpole/du Deffand correspondence in the Yale Edition of Horace Walpole's Correspondence (volumes 3 through 8.)

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Series I is organized into two subseries: Personal Correspondence, and Third-Party Correspondence.

custodhist.head: Custodial History
custodhist.p: Former call numbers include: 49 2545 I; 49 2545 IV; 49 2545 V; 49 2545 VI; LWL MSS Vol. 137

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Arrranged alphabetically by last name, followed by three bound volumes containing additional correspondence with several people who appear in the alphabetical list.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Dated the 25th day of an unknown month and year.

scopecontent.p: Items in Box 14 are photostat copies of manuscripts not owned by the Lewis Walpole Library. They may be consulted in the reading room but may not be copied or reproduced.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: On the same sheet as Voltaire's letter of 1768 July 13.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Box 2 contains a bound volume with five letters from Horace Walpole to Marie du Deffand. While many of du Deffand's letters to Walpole survive, only five from him to her, in his hand, are known to exist today. All written in French, they were acquired by Maggs Bros. at Sotheby's Parker Jervis sale in March 1920 (lot 392) and later sold to Lewis. The letters were bound by Sangorski & Sutcliffe in a full navy leather binding with a gold-tooled frame and stamped title on the front cover and raised bands on the spine. The leather turn-downs feature gold dentelle tooling. A title page and a preface that includes a brief biographical sketch, are written in red and black calligraphy. Each letter is followed by a typed English translation by an unidentified person. The volume also includes a portrait print of Horace Walpole as a frontispiece and one of du Deffand inserted opposite the preface.

custodhist.head: Custodial History
custodhist.p: Letters in Box 2: former call number 49 2545 I.

acqinfo.head: Immediate Source of Acquisition
acqinfo.p: Lot 392 at the Parker Jervis sale. Purchased by Maggs Bros.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The three volumes here contain approximately 525 letters sent between Marie du Deffand and her friends, primarily Louise Honorine Crozat, duchesse de Choiseul (1734-1801), Etienne-François, duc de Choiseul (1719-1785), l'Abbé Barthélemy (1716-1795), Stanislas-Jean de Boufflers (1738-1815), and Wilhelm Friedrich, Baron von Gleichen-Russwurm (1717-1783). Many of the letters still bear their original red or black wax seals. Some of du Deffand's outgoing letters are marked as copies, although from the address sheets it appears that some of her letters had been returned to her by the recipients.

Many letters were dated in pencil by Sainte-Aulaire, although some remained undated. The letters in each volume are arranged in chronological order followed by the replies. The letters also bear the editorial marks of Sainte-Aulaire and Mary Berry; some are numbered in pencil in the upper left corner, possibly by Sainte-Aulaire, and others have a bold red-ink numbering. In his introduction to the 1939 Yale Walpole edition of Marie's du Deffand's correspondence, Wilmarth Lewis describes the notation practices of both du Deffand editors, stating that Berry used a red-ink numbering system as part of her editorial practice.

custodhist.head: Custodial History
custodhist.p: A letter from the Paris dealer Arthur Rau to Wilmarth Lewis indicates that Rau obtained the volumes from an unidentified owner and notes that they formed the basis for Sainte-Aulaire's 1866 edition of du Deffand's correspondence. The letters have been carefully tipped on to stubs well-spaced in fine bindings of three-quarter Russia by Trautz-Bauzonnet with gold-stamped titles "Lettres Originales" on the spines, dating from about 1850, according to Rau. It is not clear, however, who commissioned the bindings.
Former call number: LWL MSS Vol. 137.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Response to Argens' letter of 1752 September 2.

did.unittitle: Letter to the King of Prussia
Letter to the King of Prussia
Argens, Jean-Baptiste de Boyer, marquis d', 1704-1771
Argenson, Marc-Pierre de Voyer, comte d', 1696-1764

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: All items in Box 12 are in the bound volume titled "Oeuvres de Mr. le Chevalier de Boufflers." Many of the letters here are to his mother, Marie Françoise Catherine de Beauvau-Craon, marquise de Boufflers (1711–1786).

custodhist.head: Custodial History
custodhist.p: Material in Box 12: former call number 49 2545 IV.

acqinfo.head: Immediate Source of Acquisition
acqinfo.p: Material in Box 12: Lot 390 in the 1920 Parker Jervis sale. Purchased from Maggs Bros., February 1933.

did.unittitle: Letter to the prince de Beauvau

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: With "Reflexions de Mr le Cher. de Boufflers."

did.unittitle: Letter to Jean-Jacques Rousseau under the guise of Emile

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: With Walpole's annotations identifying his authorship.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Series II holds one item, Marie du Deffand's manuscript journal for the years 1779-1780. The volume is bound in green vellum, which matches the "Oeuvres de Mr. le Chevalier de Boufflers" (in Series III, Box 12). There is a faded title in manuscript on the cover with a table of contents on the first page. The majority of the diary is in Jean-François Wiart's hand, and only about a quarter of the leaves in the volume have entries, which are brief and cryptic, chiefly noting the company she kept day by day. A complete transcript of the journal can be found in the Yale Walpole edition, volume 7, pages 421-461.

acqinfo.head: Immediate Source of Acquisition
acqinfo.p: Lot 390 in the 1920 Parker Jervis sale. Purchased from Maggs Bros., February 1933.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Series III holds a variety of manuscript materials, including prose, verse, songs, word portraits, documents, and other papers by du Deffand and other members of her circle. Most of the materials in this series are in Wiart's hand, and a good number of the items were annotated by Horace Walpole, as noted at the folder level.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Series III is organized into two subseries: Identified Authors, and Unidentified Authors.

did.unittitle: Adhémar, Antoine Honneste de Monteil de Brunier, marquis d', 1710-1785
"Chanson de Mad. D'ademar a sou mari"
"Envoy a Madame la marquise du Deffand sur ses ÿeux"
"Vers a Madame la marquise du Deffand sur ses ÿeux"
"Reflections sur Lusage et sur L'abus de la philosophie dans les matieres de gout par monsieur Dalambert"
"Reflexions sur 'histoire et sur le differentes manieres de l'ecrire"
"Vers au Roi de Prusse: Ainsy des aquilons, images de la guerre…"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: In an unidentified hand. Another copy in "Recueil de divers ouvrages," p. 215.

scopecontent.p: Followed by verses to members of his family. In a bound pamphlet dated 22 May in pencil on the back cover. With Walpole's annotations.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Manuscript copy in an unidentified hand. The author of these verses on Montesquieu's "The Spirit of Laws" was identified by Robert Shackleton, Bodley's Librarian, University of Oxford, in 1977.

did.unittitle: Boufflers, marquise de (Beauvau-Craon, Marie-Françoise-Catherine, princess de), 1711-1786

scopecontent.p: In Boufflers' hand. On the same sheet as the vicomte de Cambis' translation of "Caracterie de Georges premiere."

did.unittitle: "Si lon cherche un roi quón rime"
"Oeuvres de Mr. le Chevalier de Boufflers"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Box 12 contains a volume of letters and other writings by the poet Stanislas-Jean de Boufflers. It is bound in green vellum with a red morocco spine label that matches Marie du Deffand's journal in Series II, Box 11. The entire volume is in the hand of Jean-François Wiart and is annotated by Horace Walpole throughout. There are 221 filled pages numbered in manuscript and approximately 100 unnumbered blank pages, followed by a table of contents. The text in larger script was interpreted as the title of shorter pieces.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: All items in Box 12 are listed in the order they appear in the volume with the exception of letters, all of which have been listed by correspondent separately in Series I.

custodhist.head: Custodial History
custodhist.p: Material in Box 12: former call number 49 2545 IV.

acqinfo.head: Immediate Source of Acquisition
acqinfo.p: Lot 390 in the 1920 Parker Jervis sale. Purchased from Maggs Bros., February 1933.

did.unittitle: "Vers do M. Le. Cher. De Boufflers"
"M. Le Mal. De Luxembourg pour qui un demand ou deux vers"
"Impromptû au roi de pologne sure sa pipe"
"Du Meme en Envoyant son discours sur l Eloquence"
"Chanson sur l'air de mon berger volage"
"Chanson sur l'air; ah' vous dirai--ie maman"
"Chanson de Mr. Le Cher. de Boufflers a Mad. la mere qui avois fait vue chanson contre lui"
"Chanson sur l'air. En amour tous en l'angage; ou lappelle toujour panpan"
"Envoy a Mad. De Cambis sur le meme air"
"Chanson sur l'air; Jusques dans la muindre chose"
"Sur l'air; ah Pierre; ah Pierre, Je lois morte sans vous"
"Chanson sur l'air, de mon berger volage"
"A Mesdames de Lillebonne, de Monais, d'Egmont, de mesme et de choisi sur l'air Rosette &c."
"Chanson a Mad. Degmont sur l'air; Rosine si ton ame"
"A Mad. La D. de Mazarin sur l'air; ne ula til pas que J'aime"
"A Mr. de pontdevelle sur l'air: on ne savise Jamais de tour"
"Chanson a boire sur l'air dez noels"
"Quatrain mis au bas du portrait de M. de la columbiere, dessine par Mr. Le Cher. de Boufflers"
"Epitaphe de Mr. Le Cher. de Boufflers par lui meme"
"Chanson de M Le Cher. de Boufflers sur trois-dames de ses amies; dour le nom commaneurs a l une par un A, a l'autre par un B, et a la troisieme par un C."
"Chanson sur la feste du Roy de pologne au--passage de mes des de france a luneville"

"Chanson sur une charbonniere qui venoir de passer, devant le chateau de montmorency, assise sur une charrette chargeé de faes a charbous, a dans le tems que parûs L Education ou L Emilie de J.J. Rousseau"
"Epitre de M. de Voltaire a Mr. Le Cher. de Boufflers"
"Reponse de M. Le Cher. de Boufflers a M. de Voltaire"
"Chanson de M. Le Cher. de Beauvau sur Mr. Le D. De Chartres; chautè a la comedie Italienne"
"Logogriphe de Mr. Le Cher. De Boufflers adressé a une dance"
"Vers de Mr. le Cher. De Boufflers en addresant au Roi de Danemarck…"
"Du meme a la fin d'une lettre de Mad. de C. a Mr. de C."
"Envoy des fables de la fontaine a Mad. La Csse[?] de B.
"Boutes Rimes, par Mr. Le Cher. de Boufflers"
"Les memes, par Mr. Le Cher. de Beauvau"
"Epigramme de Mr. Le Cher. de Boufflers contre Mr. Le P. de Beauvau"
"Chanson faites au seminaire sur lair de la romance de vergie"
"Chanson Le Roy de pologne reprochois a lauteur de passer sa vie dan les auberges es cabarets, il repondis sur le champ. Sur lair reveille vois belle endormie"
"Chanson sur Le Mis. de bouflers sur lair jamais je noublirey mon ismene"
"Chanson sur l'air ah le belle oiseau maman"
"Vers au Roy Stanislaus pour le jour de sa fete"
"Epitre a Mr. Le p. de Beauvau"
"Vers a Mr. Le prince de beauvau qui étoit attendû dans un vieux chateau que mad. La M. de bouflers sa soeur avois meublè pouvle recevoir"
"A Mad. La p. de Craon le jour de fre anne sa feste"
"A Mad. De lenoncourt le jour de fre thèrese"
"Couplet a mad. Le M. de bouflers sur l'air, mon coeur charmé des a chaime"
"De Mr. Le Cher. de Boufflers a Mad. La V. de Cambise"
[Copy of Bourdeaux and Humain's suicide note, 25 December 1773]
[Copy of Bourdeaux's letter to his regimental lieutenant]
"Discours prononcé par Mr. de Brou a la cloture de La chambre royalle"
Cambis, Gabrielle-Charlotte Françoise d'Hénin-Liétard, vicomtesse de, 1729–1809

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: On the same sheet as S.J. de Boufflers' "Envoi de mylord Chesterfield…"

did.unittitle: Epigram for King Christian VII of Denmark

[Advertisement of sale of Vichy family timber]
"Dans les traités et dans la vie"
"Vers a Mr. Le Duc de Choiseul sur son exil"
Choiseul, Louise Honorine Crozat, duchesse de, 1734-1801
Curé de St. Jean de Latran
"Epitre de M. le Curé de St. Jean de Latran, à l'auteur de Mélanie"
"Genealogie de la maison du Bary more au morte au 1774"
"Portrait de Mad. de Gontaut par M. du Chatel"
du Deffand, Marie de Vichy Chamrond, marquise, 1697-1780

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Marie du Deffand drew many eminent figures to her salon, but she was also a careful observer of people and recorded her sometimes less than flattering impressions of those she encountered. These character "portraits" and reminiscences of her friends and acquaintances form an important portion of this collection.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: All items in Box 13 are arranged in the order they appear in the bound volume. The remaining materials in this series are arranged in alphabetical order.

custodhist.head: Custodial History
custodhist.p: Material in Box 13: former call number 49 2545 III.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Verse about an egg to the duchesse de Choiseul.

did.unittitle: Word portrait of Mme. du Deffand, by herself

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Items in Box 14 are photostat copies of manuscripts not owned by the Lewis Walpole Library. They may be consulted in the reading room but may not be copied or reproduced.

did.unittitle: "Portrait de Mr. de Pontdeveyle envoye a Mr. de Jousae"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: In an unidentified hand. According to Warren Hunting Smith, possible for the fête of Voltaire?

did.unittitle: "Quoique je forme un corps, je ne suis qu'une idée…"
"Vers sur le Prince Edward" and "Verse sur M. de Mirepoix"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Box 13 contains a volume of narrative word portraits, verses, and other writings by many of Madame du Deffand's friends and correspondents, including the duchesse de Choiseul, President Hénault, the duchesse de Luynes, the duchesse du Maine, the chevalier de Boufflers, Voltaire, and Horace Walpole, among many others. Many of the portraits were written by du Deffand or other members of her circle and others were autobiographical essays. While most of her friends seemed to take her studies of them in good humor, the evidence of excised pages and a comparison against the table of contents reveals that some of her acquaintances preferred du Deffand's opinions remain private. Interspersed among the word portraits are a dramatic scene and a one-act play, as well as a variety of verses and other short pieces.

Titles are transcribed as they appear at the heading of each piece. The volume is paginated in manuscript throughout. Although there is a table of contents in the back of the volume, some of the shorter pieces are grouped together in the table of contents under a collective title, and the decision was made to more fully represent the title of each piece as it appears in the body of the manuscript. Titles of the shorter pieces and verses are transcribed and listed here as they appear at the heading of each piece in the body of the manuscript.

Tipped in at both the beginning of the text are pages of notes in Walpole's hand including a short account of Walpole's his friendship with du Deffand and a poem he penned in 1776 extolling her wit and virtue titled "Portrait de Madame la Marquise du Deffand." Several pages of Walpole's notes are inserted at the end of the volume and offer further commentary on individuals profiled by du Deffand.

The volume contains 272 numbered pages followed by a table of contents. It is bound in a red calf binding with gold dentelle tooling and a fold-over closure with a metal clasp and green silk endpapers. The pastedowns also have flat pockets covered in the same green silk as the endpapers. The text, including the title, is in the hand of Jean-François Wiart.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: All items in Box 13 are in a bound volume and are listed in the order they appear in the volume.

custodhist.head: Custodial History
custodhist.p: This volume was part of du Deffand's papers that passed to Walpole after her death and there is evidence of his ownership and interaction with the volume throughout.

acqinfo.head: Immediate Source of Acquisition
acqinfo.p: Lot 388 in the 1920 Parker-Jervis sale. Purchased from Maggs Bros., 1938.

"Portrait de Monsieur Le Marquis de Gontault Par Madame la Mxxx de Gxxx"

"Portrait de la Princesse de Cleves par Madame La Marquise de Gontault"
"Portrait de Monsieur de Bombarde par Madame La Marquise Gontault"
"Dialogue de Made. la Mxxx de Humarous & de Ninon Lanclos par Mr. Le Pxxx Lxxx"
"Portrait de Mde. la Marquise de Mirepoix par M. le President de Montesquieu"
"Portrait de Mde. la M. de Mirepoix par M.de du Deffand"
"Portrait de Made. La Duchesse du Maine par Mad.e la Comtesse de Stal"
"Portrait de Mad.e la D. du Maine par Mad. la M. du Deffand"
"Portrait de Mad.e L... M. Du Ch'atelet"
"De Made. du Deffand pour Mad.e de Boufflers"
"Sur une petite fille nommeé Pamêla prise dans un moulin apellé Douvre sur l'air, chevalier de la table ronde"
"En voyans un evantail ou le moulin & la petite fille éntoiant representés sur l'air, vive Loüis, vive son fils"
"Autre pour Made. La D. de Boufflers sur l'air, chevalier de la table ronde"
"A Made. la D. de la Valiere pour le jour de sa feste sur l'aid des Triolets"

"Au nom de Made. de la Valiere qui donnoit a Made. de Boufflers pour le bouquet de sa feste un feu d'artifice et un pagode du japon representant une femme: sur un air de la foire, ha c'est une merveille"
"De Mr. de Voltaire, a Made. de Boufflers pour sa feste, sur l'air--des folies d'Espagne"
"De Mr. de Voltaire, en impromptu a Mr de la Valliere, sur l'air, et Hon Hon"
"Response de Mr. de la Valliere en impromptu sur l'air, vous envenés &c"
"Sur Made. La D. Dxxx sur un air nouveau"
"Chanson de Mr. Clereau sur un air de Rameau, a lamour rendés les armes &c"
"Sur Made. D------ sur l'air vous quón garde de prés &c"
"Lettre de Mr. de Formont a Made. la Marquise du Deffand qui entoit a la cour"
"Vers de Monsieur Le Pxxx Hxxx a Made. du Deffand qui disoit quelle ne voudroit pas epouser son amant afin de pouvoir l'aimer toujours par choise et jamais par devoir"
"Lettre de Mr. de Voltaire a Mr. le President Henault"
"Response de Mr. le President Henault a Mr. de Voltaire"
"Vers de Mr. Le President H--- pour Made. La Mxx D. Dxxx qui logeoit en ce tems la dans la cour du palais, me voyoit souvent Mr. Labbé gedoüin qui y logeoit aussi"
"Reponse de Mr. Labbé Gedouin a Mr. le President Henault"
"Brevet de philomule du regiment de la Calotte envoyé par Mr. Le P. Hxxx a Mr. Le Dxxx Dx S. Aignan"
"Chanson a Mad. L. D. G. qui etoit allé sux eaux de forges par M. L. P. Hxxx sur l'air il ny a que sept lieües"
"Lettre de Mr. Le President Henault a Made. La Duchesse Dumaine sous le nom de Made. de Fontaines"
"Noel de Mr. Le President Henault sur l'air de la mer rouge sur Mr. Labbé d Entrague"
"Chanson de Madame la Dxx Dumaine sur Mr. Le Cardinal de Polignac sur l'air, Les Bourgeois de Châtres"
"Couplets de Mr. Le Duc Dumaine, sur l'air ma voisine es tu facheé"
"Vers de Mr. Le M. de St. Aulaire faits dans sa vieillesse"
"Vers de Mr. de St. Aulaire á Made. La Duchesse Dumaine"
"Chanson de Mr. de St. Aulaire a Made. La Duchesse Dumaine"
"Noels de Mr. de St. Aulaire sur l'air laissés paistre vos bestes &c"
"Chanson de Made. La Mxxx du Deffand sur l'air quand Moyse fit deffense"
"Chanson de Made. La Mxxx du Dxxx sur l'air du cabin ca haha…sur Made. De Prye"
"Chanson sur l'air ou sén vont les gais Bergers"
"Vers de Monsieur de Formont a Mr. de Voltaire"
"Vers de Mr. de Voltaire sur Made. La Duchesse de la Valliere"
"Vers de Pirron sur L'abbé des Fontaines"
"Chanson de Mr. de Voltaire sur l'air Le Brave Comte de Tallard &c"
"Chanson de Mr. Le Grand Prieur sur Mr. Le Duc de Villars sur laid, de la sarabande D'Issé"
"Vers de Pirron sur Mr. de Voltaire"
"Logogryphe par Monsieur L'abbé de Chamron adressé a Madame La Marquise du Deffand"

"Vers de M. Dalembert sur M. Le Duc de Boufflers"

"Vers de M. Desaleurs a Mad. De Prie"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The subseries includes historical documents, character portraits, verses, and songs. Also present are a group of papers relating to Jacqueline and Jeanneton, two young women who became the wards of Marie-Françoise-Renée de Carbonnel de Canisy, comtesse de Forcalquier (1725-1796) and were supported financially by several wealthy patrons of her circle, including Marie du Deffand.

did.unittitle: "Extrait de la Garette anglaise appellée evening-post out Poste du foir"
"Relation de L'assasins du Roy du 5 janvier 1757"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This group of papers relates to the lives and care of Madame de Forcalquier's wards Jacqueline Pourblé and Jeanneton Milet. The two young women were close friends raised in the bourgeoise class but fallen into poverty after the deaths of their parents. Marie-Françoise-Renée de Carbonnel de Canisy, comtesse de Forcalquier (1725-1796) learned of their situation through one of her servants and took up their cause, petitioning her wealthy friends to contribute support for the pair. Marie du Deffand offered financial and material assistance and endeavored to raise money from her aristocratic friends.

This collection holds sixteen items relating to the two women, which were numbered and annotated by Yale editor Warren Hunting Smith. Correspondence about Jacqueline and Jeanneton is filed by correspondent in Series I and can be found by searching the finding aid for "Jeanneton." Octavie Guichard Durey de Meinières' (1719-1804) account of the girls' friendship is in Series III, Box 9, folder 27. Five miscellaneous documents that Smith numbered 5, 12, 13, 15, and 16 are filed below.

A more comprehensive story of the two young women is told in Wilmarth Lewis's Le Triomphe de l'Amitié, ou, l'Histoire de Jacqueline et de Jeanneton (Farmington, CT: 1935), which was published as his Miscellaneous Antiquities No. 10.

did.unittitle: Verses to Madame du Deffand on Jacqueline et Jeanneton
"Etat des meubles. Habits et linge donné a Jeanneton et Jacqueline"
"Memoire des au mones faites aux deux filles Jacqueline et Jeanneton"
[List of articles purchased for Jacqueline and Jeanneton, with prices]
"Traduction d'une pretendue lettre de Mad. Louise a Mad. La Dauphine en 1770"

scopecontent.p: In an unidentified hand. Translation of a pretended letter from Princesse Louise to Marie Antoinette.

did.unittitle: "Ecrit de Junius, h s'annonce par ce préambule"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: A collection of 13 miscellaneous pieces owned by du Deffand. Some in Wiart's hand; some in unidentified hands.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: A collection of 35 miscellaneous pieces, one with Walpole's annotation. Most in Wiart's hand; some in unidentified hands. These papers were so designated by Warren Hunting Smith.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: A collection of 50 miscellaneous pieces of verse and prose. Most in Wiart's hand; some in unidentified hands. In a wrapper with an annotation identifying the contents.
